1: The linen waistband (ezor pishta) pressed against YHWH's loins — the most intimate garment — is the sign-act of Israel's covenantal purpose: to cling to YHWH as a waistband clings to the body (v11). In the NT, the covenant-garment metaphor is transformed: believers 'put on Christ' (Galatians 3:27; Romans 13:14), Christ becomes the garment of righteousness. The sign-act of the ruined waistband is reversed by the new-covenant garment that cannot be corrupted.
9: 'Thus will I ruin the pride of Judah and the great pride of Jerusalem.' The destruction of national pride is the condition for receiving grace. The Beatitudes begin 'Blessed are the poor in spirit' (Matthew 5:3) — the bankruptcy of self-reliance that the ruined waistband enacts is the prerequisite for the kingdom. Christ brings not the reinforcement of human pride but its deconstruction as the path to covenant restoration.
11: 'For as the waistband clings to the loins of a man, so I made the whole house of Israel and all of Judah cling to me, declares YHWH, that they might be for me a people, a name, a praise, and a glory.' The original covenant purpose — intimate adhesion to YHWH, being his glory and praise — is what sin has destroyed and Christ comes to restore. The church as 'a chosen race, a royal priesthood, a holy nation' (1 Peter 2:9) is the restored covenant people whose clinging to Christ fulfills what the linen waistband could not.
13: 'I will fill with drunkenness all the inhabitants of this land.' The divine wine of judgment — here judgment on covenant-breakers — becomes in the NT the Supper's wine that is the blood of the new covenant (Matthew 26:27-28). The cup that the wicked receive as wrath (Revelation 14:10) is the same cup Christ drains for his people (Mark 10:38; Jeremiah 14:36), converting the judgment-cup into the covenant-cup.
14: 'I will not pity or spare or have compassion, that I should not destroy them.' The suspended compassion that Jeremiah announces as judgment on the unrepentant is the necessary backdrop for Paul's declaration: 'While we were yet sinners, Christ died for us' (Romans 5:8). Compassion is not merely withheld — it is channeled through the cross, where the wrath that would destroy the unrepentant falls on the Son, allowing compassion to flow to the repentant.
15: 'Hear and give ear; be not proud, for YHWH has spoken.' The repeated call to humble hearing throughout Jeremiah finds its NT fulfillment in Christ as the One to whom all must listen: 'This is my beloved Son; listen to him' (Mark 9:7; cf. Deuteronomy 18:15 — the prophet like Moses). The pride that refuses to listen is the same pride that puts the Son to death; the humility that hears becomes salvation.
16: 'Give glory to YHWH your God before he brings darkness, before your feet stumble on the twilight mountains.' The call to give glory before judgment — before the darkness closes in — is the missionary urgency of the NT. In Revelation 14:7, the angel flying in midheaven calls: 'Fear God and give him glory, for the hour of his judgment has come.' Christ opens the window of grace; the call to glorify YHWH before darkness is the shape of every evangelistic appeal.
17: 'But if you will not listen, my soul will weep in secret for your pride.' Jeremiah's secret weeping over the pride that will bring destruction is an unmistakable type of Christ's weeping over Jerusalem (Luke 19:41-44): 'When he drew near and saw the city, he wept over it, saying, “Would that you, even you, had known on this day the things that make for peace!”' Both Jeremiah and Jesus weep over the same stubborn city for the same fatal pride.
23: 'Can the Ethiopian change his skin or the leopard his spots? Then also you can do good who are accustomed to do evil.' The impossibility of self-reformation — the entrenched habit of sin that cannot be self-corrected — is the problem Christ addresses not by human self-improvement but by new creation: 'If anyone is in Christ, he is a new creation; the old has passed away; behold, the new has come' (2 Corinthians 5:17). What the leopard cannot do, the Spirit effects.
25: 'This is your lot, the portion I have measured out to you, declares YHWH, because you have forgotten me and trusted in lies.' Forgetting YHWH — the covenant amnesia that produces idolatrous trust in lies — is addressed by Christ who calls himself 'the truth' (John 14:6). The Spirit he sends is the Spirit of truth who will 'bring to your remembrance all that I have said to you' (John 14:26) — the covenantal remembering that reverses Jeremiah's covenantal forgetting.
27: 'Woe to you, O Jerusalem! How long will it be before you are made clean?' The closing lament — the city that cannot cleanse itself — anticipates both John the Baptist's baptism of repentance and Christ's declaration 'I will; be clean' to the leper (Mark 1:41). The impossibility of self-cleansing that Jeremiah laments is resolved by the one who washes his disciples' feet (John 13:8-10) and whose blood cleanses from all sin (1 John 1:7).
2: A theme: the first stage of the linen sash sign-act — buying and wearing the sash according to YHWH’s command. The linen sash (ʾēzôr) was the undergarment worn against the skin — an intimate article of clothing. The sign-act begins with perfect obedience: “I bought the sash, as the LORD directed.” Christ’s obedience follows the same pattern: “I have come down from heaven, not to do my own will but the will of him who sent me” (John 6:38). The prophet who acts at YHWH’s command models the Servant who “learned obedience through what he suffered” (Heb 5:8).
3: A theme: the second divine word comes to Jeremiah. The sign-act is multi-staged, requiring repeated divine instruction. God’s pedagogy through enacted parables — commanding his prophet to live out the message — anticipates Christ’s own use of enacted signs: the cleansing of the temple, the cursing of the fig tree, the foot-washing. The prophet’s life becomes a living parable of judgment and mercy. In this sense, Jeremiah’s prophetic career is a corporate “type” that Christ fulfills — not one enacted sign but an entire life that enacts the word of God.
4: A theme: hiding the sash at the Euphrates — YHWH’s people hidden and ruined among the nations. The Euphrates is the emblem of exile — Babylon lies along it. The sash is to be buried there, symbolizing that Israel will be “hidden” in Babylon, spoiled by exile. Christ uses the hiding-and-finding motif in his parables: the treasure hidden in a field (Matt 13:44), the lost coin (Luke 15:8-10). The thing that is hidden is the thing of greatest value — found by the one who seeks diligently. The “hidden” people of Israel, spoiled in Babylon, are found and restored by the one who sought the lost sheep (Luke 15:4-7).
5: A theme: “so I went and hid it at the Euphrates, as the LORD commanded.” Jeremiah’s complete obedience to the enacted sign — including the physical act of travel to the Euphrates (whether literal or visionary) — underlines the cost of prophetic obedience. The prophets did not merely speak; they lived the message. Christ embodies this principle at the highest possible level: the incarnation is the ultimate enactment of the divine message, the Word made flesh dwelling among us (John 1:14). YHWH does not merely describe exile; he sends his prophet to enact it. The Father does not merely describe redemption; he sends his Son to accomplish it.
6: A theme: the divine command to retrieve the ruined sash — “go to the Euphrates; retrieve the sash I told you to hide there.” The retrieval is the return from exile, the restoration after judgment. This two-stage movement — hiding (exile) and retrieval (restoration) — is the deep structure of the exile-return narrative. Christ himself enters the “exile” of death and is retrieved/raised: the resurrection is YHWH retrieving from death the one he had given over. The resurrection as the divine act of recovery mirrors the retrieve-the-sash command: “you will not abandon my soul to Hades, or let your Holy One see corruption” (Acts 2:27).
7: A direct anticipation: “I took the sash from the place where I had hidden it — and the sash was ruined; it was no longer useful for anything.” The sash ruined by burial — unable to serve its purpose of clinging to YHWH — is a type of Israel in exile, marred and made useless through covenant unfaithfulness. In Christ, the ruined humanity is restored: “if anyone is in Christ, he is a new creation; the old has passed away; behold, the new has come” (2 Cor 5:17). The sash that was useless is restored not by the sash reforming itself but by the divine act of re-creation in Christ — the ruined garment replaced by the robe of righteousness (Isa 61:10; Rev 3:5).
8: A theme: the word of YHWH comes again, interpreting the sign. The pattern of action followed by divine interpretation is the structure of prophetic sign-acts: the act is performed, then YHWH explains its meaning. Christ uses this same pattern — healing, then teaching; feeding, then explaining. John’s Gospel in particular organizes around “signs” followed by interpretive discourses. The structure of enacted parable (sign-act) followed by divine word is one of the deep continuities between Jeremiah’s prophetic method and Jesus’s teaching method.
10: A theme: “this wicked people who refuse to listen to my words, who follow the stubbornness of their heart... will be like that ruined sash, good for nothing.” The interpretive conclusion of the sash sign-act applies the image to the spiritually hardened community. Christ identifies the same hardness: “Though they have seen all these signs, they still would not believe” (John 12:37); and attributes it to the prophetically predicted hardening (Isaiah 6:10; John 12:40). The sash good for nothing is the person who refuses to cling to YHWH — and the only remedy is not reformation of the old sash but new creation in Christ, who makes all things new.
12: A shadow: “every wineskin will be filled with wine.” The people’s response (“we know that”) treats this as a truism, but YHWH transforms it into a judgment metaphor: they will be filled with the wine of confusion and staggering. Christ uses the new wine / new wineskin image to describe his own coming: “No one puts new wine into old wineskins; if he does, the new wine will burst the wineskins” (Luke 5:37). The wine of Jeremiah’s judgment-filling (drunken staggering) contrasts with the new wine of the kingdom (joy and abundance at the wedding feast, John 2:1-11; Rev 19:9).
18: A direct anticipation: “Say to the king and to the queen mother: Take a lower place and sit down; your positions of authority will come down off your heads.” The humiliation of the Davidic king — Jehoiachin and his mother before Nebuchadnezzar — is part of the low point of the Davidic line that Christ reverses. Jesus is born into the humbled Davidic line: no throne, no palace, “no place to lay his head” (Luke 9:58), born to a young woman in an occupied land. Yet this humiliation is the path to the exaltation: “God highly exalted him and gave him the name that is above every name” (Phil 2:9). The crown taken off at Jeremiah 13:18 is the crown that Christ restores in glory.
19: A theme: “The towns of the Negev will be locked shut with no one to open them; all of Judah will be taken into exile.” The total exile — no one left to open the locked towns — is the nadir of the Davidic covenant. Christ is the key of David: “the holy one, the true one, who has the key of David, who opens and no one will shut, who shuts and no one opens” (Rev 3:7). The locked towns of Jeremiah’s judgment are the shadow of every door closed by human sin; Christ is the one who opens what no one can shut — beginning with the sealed tomb from which he rises.
20: A theme: “Where is the flock that was entrusted to you — the beautiful flock?” The scattered flock is the people of God in the hour of their judgment. Christ the Good Shepherd answers this question definitively: “I am the good shepherd; I know my own and my own know me” (John 10:14); “I lay down my life for the sheep” (John 10:15). The beautiful flock scattered in Jeremiah’s day is gathered by the Shepherd who leaves the ninety-nine to find the one lost sheep (Luke 15:4-7), ultimately laying down his life to gather the flock that had been entrusted to him.
21: A theme: “What will you say when he punishes you — when those you trained as your allies are placed over you as head?” The reversal — trusted allies become masters — is the logic of covenant curse (Deuteronomy 28:44). Christ’s kingdom operates by the same reversal-logic but in grace: the first will be last and the last first (Matt 20:16); the one who humbles himself will be exalted (Luke 14:11). Where Jeremiah’s reversal is punitive (Judah’s allies become her rulers), Christ’s reversal is redemptive (the servant becomes the heir, Gal 4:7).
22: A theme: “it is because of the magnitude of your iniquity that your skirts were lifted up.” The exposure of shame as covenant penalty appears throughout the prophets (Ezek 16; Hos 2). Christ provides the covering for human shame: “I counsel you to buy from me white garments so that you may clothe yourself and the shame of your nakedness may not be seen” (Rev 3:18). The shame exposed in Jeremiah’s judgment is the same shame that Christ covers at the cross — putting on himself the nakedness of crucifixion so that his people might be clothed in the righteousness he earned (2 Cor 5:21; Isa 61:10).
24: A theme: “I will scatter them like chaff blown away by a desert wind.” The winnowing image — separating wheat from chaff, the genuine from the worthless — runs from the prophets to John the Baptist to Christ: “his winnowing fork is in his hand, and he will clear his threshing floor, gathering his wheat into the barn and burning up the chaff with unquenchable fire” (Matt 3:12). The scattering by desert wind in Jeremiah 13 is a preview of the eschatological winnowing at which Christ presides. The same Christ who is himself the grain of wheat that falls into the earth and dies (John 12:24) will gather the final harvest and separate chaff from grain.
26: A theme: “so I will pull your skirts up over your face and expose your shameful nakedness.” The prophetic use of exposure imagery as covenant judgment is widespread (Ezek 16; Nahum 3:5). It speaks of the stripping away of the pretensions of the covenant people who have acted as faithless. Christ on the cross enacts the ultimate exposure of shame — stripped of his garments while soldiers cast lots (John 19:23-24; Ps 22:18) — not because of his own shame but to bear the shame of others. His exposure becomes the covering: “put on the Lord Jesus Christ” (Rom 13:14) is Paul’s application of the garment-theology that runs from Genesis through Jeremiah to the cross.
